examples.title: Algorithm: Round Robin CPU Scheduling
examples.description: Demonstrates how the OS creates the illusion of multitasking by time-slicing CPU access among multiple processes
examples.code:
ALGORITHM RoundRobinScheduling
INPUT: List of processes, Time Quantum
OUTPUT: Execution order and waiting times

BEGIN
 // Initialize
 SET readyQueue = empty
 SET currentTime = 0
 SET completed = 0
 SET n = number of processes
 
 // Add all processes to ready queue (arrival time 0)
 FOR each process in processList:
 ADD process TO readyQueue
 SET remainingTime[process] = burstTime[process]
 END FOR
 
 // Main scheduling loop
 WHILE completed < n:
 // If ready queue is empty, jump to next arrival
 IF readyQueue is empty:
 SET currentTime = next process arrival time
 ADD next process TO readyQueue
 CONTINUE
 END IF
 
 // Get next process from queue
 SET currentProcess = REMOVE first FROM readyQueue
 
 // Execute for time quantum or until completion
 SET executeTime = MIN(timeQuantum, remainingTime[currentProcess])
 SET remainingTime[currentProcess] = remainingTime[currentProcess] - executeTime
 SET currentTime = currentTime + executeTime
 
 // Check if process completed
 IF remainingTime[currentProcess] == 0:
 SET completionTime[currentProcess] = currentTime
 SET waitingTime[currentProcess] = completionTime[currentProcess] - arrivalTime[currentProcess] - burstTime[currentProcess]
 SET completed = completed + 1
 ELSE:
 // Add back to ready queue
 ADD currentProcess TO readyQueue
 END IF
 
 // Add newly arrived processes to queue
 FOR each process in processList:
 IF arrivalTime[process] <= currentTime AND process NOT in readyQueue AND process NOT completed:
 ADD process TO readyQueue
 END IF
 END FOR
 END WHILE
 
 // Calculate and display results
 DISPLAY "Process ID\tWaiting Time"
 FOR each process:
 DISPLAY process.id, waitingTime[process]
 END FOR
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the Round Robin CPU scheduling algorithm, which is one of the fundamental ways an OS creates the illusion of multitasking. The CPU scheduler gives each process a small time slice (quantum) to execute, then moves to the next process. This time-slicing creates the appearance that multiple programs run simultaneously even though the CPU can only execute one instruction at a time.

examples.title: Python: Process Management Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates OS process management including creation, scheduling, and context switching
examples.code:
import time
import queue
import random
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Process:
 """Represents a process in the OS"""
 pid: int
 name: str
 burst_time: int # Time needed to complete
 arrival_time: int
 priority: int
 remaining_time: int = 0
 waiting_time: int = 0
 turnaround_time: int = 0
 
 def __post_init__(self):
 self.remaining_time = self.burst_time

class OperatingSystem:
 """Simulates basic OS process management functions"""
 
 def __init__(self):
 self.ready_queue = queue.Queue() # Processes waiting for CPU
 self.completed_processes = []
 self.current_time = 0
 self.context_switch_time = 1
 
 def create_process(self, name: str, burst_time: int, priority: int = 1) -> Process:
 """Process creation - OS allocates memory and creates PCB"""
 pid = len(self.completed_processes) + len(self.ready_queue.queue) + 100
 process = Process(
 pid=pid,
 name=name,
 burst_time=burst_time,
 arrival_time=self.current_time,
 priority=priority
 )
 print(f"[OS] Created process {process.name} (PID: {pid})")
 print(f" Allocated memory block: {pid * 1000} - {(pid+1)*1000 - 1}")
 return process
 
 def add_to_ready_queue(self, process: Process):
 """Add process to the ready queue for scheduling"""
 self.ready_queue.put(process)
 print(f"[OS] Added {process.name} to ready queue")
 
 def schedule_fcfs(self):
 """First-Come-First-Served scheduling algorithm"""
 print("\n" + "="*50)
 print("Starting FCFS Scheduling")
 print("="*50 + "\n")
 
 while not self.ready_queue.empty():
 # CPU Scheduler selects next process
 current_process = self.ready_queue.get()
 
 print(f"[SCHEDULER] Dispatching {current_process.name} to CPU")
 print(f"[CPU] Executing {current_process.name} for {current_process.remaining_time} units...")
 
 # Simulate execution
 time.sleep(min(current_process.remaining_time, 0.5))
 self.current_time += current_process.remaining_time
 
 # Process completion
 current_process.turnaround_time = self.current_time - current_process.arrival_time
 current_process.waiting_time = current_process.turnaround_time - current_process.burst_time
 
 self.completed_processes.append(current_process)
 print(f"[OS] Process {current_process.name} completed!")
 print(f" Turnaround time: {current_process.turnaround_time}")
 print(f" Waiting time: {current_process.waiting_time}\n")
 
 # Context switch overhead
 if not self.ready_queue.empty():
 self.current_time += self.context_switch_time
 print(f"[OS] Context switch: +{self.context_switch_time} time units\n")
 
 def display_memory_status(self):
 """Display memory allocation (simplified)"""
 print("\n[MEMORY MANAGER] Current Memory Status:")
 print("-" * 40)
 total_memory = 4096 # 4MB simulated
 used = len(self.completed_processes) * 1000
 free = total_memory - used
 print(f"Total Memory: {total_memory} KB")
 print(f"Used: {used} KB")
 print(f"Free: {free} KB")

# Demo: Simulate process management
if __name__ == "__main__":
 os = OperatingSystem()
 
 # Create multiple processes (simulating user launching apps)
 processes_data = [
 ("Web Browser", 3, 2),
 ("Word Processor", 2, 3),
 ("Music Player", 4, 1),
 ]
 
 for name, burst, priority in processes_data:
 proc = os.create_process(name, burst, priority)
 os.add_to_ready_queue(proc)
 
 # Memory status after creating processes
 os.display_memory_status()
 
 # Run the scheduler
 os.schedule_fcfs()
 
 # Final statistics
 print("="*50)
 print("FINAL STATISTICS")
 print("="*50)
 total_waiting = sum(p.waiting_time for p in os.completed_processes)
 print(f"Average Waiting Time: {total_waiting / len(os.completed_processes):.2f} time units")
examples.explanation: This Python simulation demonstrates three key OS functions: 1) Process Creation - the OS creates processes and allocates memory (PID assignment); 2) Process Scheduling - the CPU scheduler (FCFS algorithm) decides which process runs; 3) Context Switching - the overhead when moving between processes. The ready queue represents the set of processes waiting for CPU time, demonstrating how the OS manages the multitasking illusion.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of processes

examples.title: C: Process Creation with fork()
examples.description: Demonstrates OS process management using the fork() system call to create new processes
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates OS process management using the fork() system call, which is fundamental to how Unix/Linux operating systems create new processes. The fork() system call duplicates the current process, creating a child process with its own memory space. The parent uses wait() to collect the child's exit status, preventing zombie processes. This shows how the OS manages process creation, execution, and cleanup - essential functions of the Process Management component.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for fork, O(n) for n process creations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is size of process memory space
